concepts.definition: When you push down on a beam, the top edge gets squeezed shorter and the bottom edge gets stretched longer; the internal push-back per unit of cross-section area that resists this is the bending stress.

unpacked.bending stress: internal push-back force spread over each bit of cross-section area

workedExample.content: A 2 m steel ruler clamped at one end with a 1 kg weight at the tip: the top-surface fibers shorten, the bottom fibers lengthen, and the stress is largest at the clamped end where the bending is greatest.

concepts.restatementPrompt: In your own words, what happens to the top and bottom of the beam, and where is the stress largest?

concepts.definition: A number that says how much a beam cross-section shape resists bending, just from how its material is spread out away from the center line: material far from the center fights bending much harder.

unpacked.second moment of area: how the cross-section area is distributed relative to the bending axis; far-out material counts a lot more

workedExample.content: An I-beam puts most of its steel in the top and bottom flanges, far from the center, so for the same weight of steel it resists sagging far more than a solid square bar of the same area.

concepts.restatementPrompt: Explain, in your own words, why an I-beam resists sag better than a square bar of the same material.

concepts.definition: The tip sag of a loaded beam grows with the load and very fast with length, and shrinks as the material gets stiffer and the cross-section spreads its material farther out.

unpacked.deflection: how far the beam moves from its straight resting position

workedExample.content: For a cantilever with a tip load, sag = F*L^3/(3*E*I): doubling the length L makes it sag 8x more (2^3), while doubling I (a stiffer shape) halves the sag, so a beam twice as long needs 8x the stiffness to sag the same amount.

concepts.restatementPrompt: Without using the formula, explain why a beam twice as long sags far more than just twice as much.

gradings.followUpQuestion: What makes the bottom fibers stretch rather than squeeze?
Where would this length-cubed rule stop holding?

turns.recoveryPrompt: Now explain, in your own words, why the exponent is 3 and not 1.
